name: Coal Hollow
short_description: Collect quartz, calcite, and fluorite specimens in Martin County's gentle rolling terrain. This accessible site offers fossils and mineral deposits typical of Indiana's Carboniferous limestone form...
description:
Coal Hollow in Martin County offers rockhounders access to minerals found in Indiana's rich Carboniferous-age limestone and shale deposits. The site sits at 772 feet elevation with gentle slopes ideal for family collecting. Visitors can search for quartz crystals, calcite specimens, and fluorite in weathered outcrops and creek washes. The area's geological history as a shallow inland sea 300 million years ago created abundant fossil material and mineral-rich rock layers that persist today. Local collectors appreciate the moderate terrain and productive collecting without the need for technical equipment or extensive hiking.

The gentle slope terrain makes Coal Hollow suitable for beginners and families seeking an introduction to Midwestern rockhounding. Creek beds and eroded hillsides expose fresh material regularly, especially after spring rains. The site maintains reasonable remoteness while remaining accessible to visitors from nearby towns. Fluorite specimens here can range from clear to purple, and calcite often displays excellent crystalline structure. Petrified wood pieces are also found, remnants of ancient forests preserved in the mineral-rich sedimentary layers.

Because access details for this location require verification with local landowners and current regulations, visiting rockhounds should confirm permission before collecting. The surrounding Martin County area contains numerous small mineral-bearing formations, and Coal Hollow represents one accessible collecting opportunity in this geologically productive region. Spring through fall typically offers the best conditions for field access.
history: Coal Hollow's mineral deposits formed during the Carboniferous Period (approximately 320 million years ago) when Indiana was covered by shallow inland seas. Limestone, shale, and mineral-rich sediments accumulated in these marine environments, and later groundwater carrying dissolved minerals percolated through the rock layers, depositing fluorite, calcite, and quartz crystals in fractures and cavities. This region's coal-bearing formations gave rise to the name Coal Hollow and reflect the organic-rich sediments typical of Pennsylvanian-age deposits across southern Indiana.
tips: Search creek beds and eroded hillside banks where fresh material is constantly exposed by water action and weathering; fluorite specimens often weather out in clusters along stream channels. Use a small flathead chisel or pry bar to gently remove specimens from soft shale, being careful not to damage crystal faces. Look for purple or colorless fluorite cubes, clear to milky quartz points, and rhombohedral calcite crystals; specimens here typically range from thumbnail to 3 inches. Bring a mesh bag or cloth pouch to protect delicate crystals during transport, and examine loose material carefully as small specimens are easily overlooked.
terrain_notes: Elevation 772 feet with gentle rolling slopes accessible to most hikers. Uneven rocky terrain with exposed limestone outcrops and stream banks. Sturdy footwear recommended for walking creek beds. Vegetation includes typical Indiana deciduous forest with occasional clearings. Walking distance from parking estimated under 1 mile to productive collecting areas.
best_season: April - October. Spring through early fall offers optimal conditions when creek water levels expose fresh material after winter and spring rains. Winter access can be difficult due to muddy terrain. Summer heat is moderate in this region; bring water and sun protection.
written_directions: From Shoals (the nearest town), head south on County Road 550 for approximately 4 miles toward the Martin County mining area. Coal Hollow lies east of the main road near spring-fed creeks in the gently sloping terrain. Local knowledge or inquiries at Shoals may provide more precise access points. Verify with landowners before entering private property.
road_conditions: County roads to area; unpaved local roads may be muddy during wet seasons
parking_notes: Informal pullout parking near access points; no developed parking lot. Verify parking locations with property owners.
collection_rules: Private land - permission required from property owner before collecting. Respect posted no trespassing signs. Remove only what you can carry. No motorized excavation or large-scale mining. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Obtain permission from landowner before entering
No motorized equipment or heavy excavation
Collect reasonable amounts for personal use only
Respect property boundaries and posted signs
Pack out all collected material and trash
nearest_services: Shoals, Indiana approximately 4 miles north - gas, food, and basic supplies available
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers. Creek beds require careful footing but no technical climbing needed. Unpaved road to area may have muddy sections. Suitable for families with children ages 6 and up. Limited wheelchair accessibility due to rough, uneven terrain.

faq.question: What minerals can I find rockhounding at Coal Hollow in Indiana?
faq.answer: Coal Hollow is known for quartz crystals, calcite specimens, and purple or colorless fluorite, along with occasional petrified wood pieces. The fluorite here often forms small cubes and clusters found in eroded creek beds and shale outcrops. Calcite displays excellent rhombohedral crystal structure typical of Carboniferous limestone formations.

faq.question: Is Coal Hollow rockhounding site free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Coal Hollow is located on private land, so there is no fee. However, you must obtain permission from the property owner before collecting. Always verify access and confirm you have permission before visiting. Contact local Shoals residents or Martin County rockhounding groups for current access information.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Coal Hollow, Indiana?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal. Spring and early summer rains expose fresh material in creek beds. Winter can be muddy and difficult to access. Summer heat is manageable in this region. Avoid late fall and winter when weather and wet terrain make collecting challenging.

faq.question: Do I need special permits or tools to collect at Coal Hollow?
faq.answer: No permit is required, but permission from the landowner is essential. Bring a small flathead chisel, pry bar, mesh collecting bag, and sturdy boots for creek walking. A hand lens helps identify fluorite and calcite specimens. Most collectors find handheld tools sufficient for removing specimens from soft shale.

faq.question: Is Coal Hollow rockhounding good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Coal Hollow is beginner-friendly with easy to moderate terrain and no technical climbing required. The gentle slopes and accessible creek beds suit families with children ages 6 and up. Specimens like fluorite and calcite are easy to identify, making it ideal for learning basic mineral recognition skills.

faq.question: How far is Coal Hollow from Shoals, and what directions should I use to get there?
faq.answer: Coal Hollow is approximately 4 miles south of Shoals. Head south on County Road 550 toward the Martin County mining area. The site lies in gently rolling terrain near spring-fed creeks. Confirm exact access and parking with local property owners before visiting, as access may be seasonal or restricted.
